title: Michael J. White and Jennifer E. Glick: Achieving Anew. How New Immigrants Do in American Schools, Jobs, and Neighborhoods
abstract: In this book, Michael J. White and Jennifer E. Glick aim at providing a comprehensive picture of structural assimilation of immigrants in contemporary America. Their focus is on ‘patterns of adaptation linked to socio-economic position in American society’ (172), that is, on educational achievement, labour market outcomes, and residential intermingling. As by definition immigrant assimilation takes time, a longitudinal viewpoint is adapted, both by comparing immigrant generations (first, one-and-a-half, second, or third-plus), cohorts, and by analysing truly longitudinal individual panel data. After introducing the topic in Chapter 1, the second chapter provides an assessment of the scale of contemporary immigration as compared to U.S. immigration waves from around 1800 onwards. The chapter discusses demographic dynamics, urban concentration of immigrants, changes in the national origins of immigrants, and gives a cross-national description of the extent to which America is an immigration country. Chapter 3, then, extensively discusses assimilation theory by reviewing branches of literature addressing straight-line assimilation, super achievement, and segmented assimilation. Most original in their theoretical standpoint is that irrespective of which theory one adheres to, to be able to test hypotheses derived from them, it is absolutely crucial to separate starting point from trajectory. That is, the relative position at one time of immigrant groups or individuals needs to be distinguished from the level of achievement since that time. Snapshots of cross-sections are always untraceable combinations of starting point and trajectory, which make them imperfect for purposes of hypothesis testing. This notion, combined with testing propositions with high-quality data sources that actually do follow individuals over time (the High School and Beyond survey, 1980 and the National Education Longitudinal Study, 1990) is the most obvious contribution that the book makes. Chapter 4 summarizes the American government’s standpoints in terms of immigration and immigrant policy. Chapters 5 to 7 present the empirical findings for the three structural indicators of assimilation. In the concluding chapter these findings are summarized, other realms of structural assimilation are discussed briefly, as are the implications for assimilation theory and policy.
